content.problemStatement:
In honor of International Justice Day, the Supreme Court of Chefland has decided to address all pending cases simultaneously.

For a given case, the accused will be convicted if the convincing power of the prosecution, denoted by integer XXX, is greater than or equal to the convincing power of the defense, denoted by integer YYY.

Determine whether the accused is convicted.


Input Format

The only line of input consists of space-separated integers XXX and YYY, denoting the convincing powers of prosecution and defense, respectively.


Output Format
Output on a new line, `YES`, if the accused is convicted and `NO` otherwise.


You may print each character of the string in uppercase or lowercase (for example, the strings `YES`, `yEs`, `yes`, and `yeS` will all be treated as identical).
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two space‑separated integers X and Y, the convincing powers of the prosecution and the defense respectively.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line "YES" if X ≥ Y (the accused is convicted) or "NO" otherwise.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Prosecution power (5) is greater than defense power (4), so the accused is convicted.
Prosecution power (1) is less than defense power (10), so the accused is not convicted.
Both powers are equal; conviction occurs when X ≥ Y.

solution.approach: For each test case we simply compare the two given integers. If the prosecution's power X is greater than or equal to the defense's power Y we print YES, otherwise we print NO. This is O(1) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work for each of the T test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few variables are used regardless of input size.
